PHINIA Inc. struck an upbeat tone on its latest earnings call, highlighting solid revenue growth, robust cash generation, and aggressive capital returns to shareholders. Management balanced this optimism with realism about margin pressure from FX, tariffs, mix and regional softness, offering a cautiously constructive outlook that leans on share gains rather than booming end markets.

Quarterly Revenue Growth

PHINIA reported Q4 net sales of $889 million, up 6.7% year over year, signaling healthy top-line momentum despite a mixed macro backdrop. Stripping out foreign exchange and the SEM acquisition, underlying revenue growth was 2.3%, showing that most of the gain came from the core business rather than currency tailwinds.

Improved Profitability Metrics in Quarter

Adjusted EBITDA in Q4 rose to $116 million, up $6 million from a year earlier, with margin holding at a solid 13%. Adjusted earnings per diluted share surged to $1.18 from $0.71, reflecting better profitability and a leaner share count after aggressive buybacks.

Segment Strength — Fuel Systems and Aftermarket

Fuel Systems delivered Q4 sales of $560 million, up 7.9%, alongside a 10.7% adjusted operating margin, underscoring resilience in the core combustion technology franchise. Aftermarket posted $329 million in sales, up 4.8%, with a strong 15.8% margin, marking the third straight quarter of growth for both segments.

Full-Year Revenue and Cash Generation

For the full year, adjusted revenue reached about $3.5 billion, up roughly 3% once certain contract manufacturing impacts are excluded, confirming steady but not explosive growth. Cash flow from operations came in at $312 million and adjusted free cash flow hit $212 million, topping guidance and reinforcing PHINIA’s cash-generation credentials.

Strong Capital Return and Balance Sheet

Since the spin, PHINIA has returned more than $500 million to shareholders via buybacks and dividends, including the repurchase of about 9.8 million shares, or roughly 21% of the original float. The balance sheet remains solid, with Q4 cash of $359 million, total liquidity of $859 million, net leverage easing to 1.3 times, and $24 million of debt repaid.

Strategic Wins and Product/Market Expansion

Management highlighted a series of strategic wins, including a third aerospace and defense contract for a post-combustion fuel valve and extensions with major commercial vehicle makers. PHINIA also secured a new CNG port fuel injector program in India, added about 5,800 new aftermarket SKUs, and advanced its aerospace capabilities through a first acquisition and key quality certification.

Tax Rate Improvement

The company significantly improved its tax profile, cutting the adjusted effective tax rate from 41.5% to 32.5%, which directly supports net income and EPS. Cash taxes paid fell to $61 million from $94 million, though management noted some of the 2025 benefits were one-offs and not all will repeat at the same scale.

Full-Year EBITDA Flat and Margin Compression

Despite revenue growth, full-year adjusted EBITDA was flat at $478 million, reflecting cost and mix pressures across the portfolio. The EBITDA margin slipped 40 basis points to 13.7%, underscoring that profitability did not fully keep pace with sales and remains a key focus area.

Margin Headwinds from FX, SEM Inclusion and Mix

Q4 margins were diluted by foreign exchange, the inclusion of SEM at lower margins, and less favorable product mix, leading to about a 20 basis point quarterly margin decline. For the full year, FX and tariffs each shaved roughly 20 basis points off margins, limiting the flow-through of growth to the bottom line.

Regional and Mix Challenges

PHINIA faced softer sales in Europe within Fuel Systems and somewhat weaker commercial vehicle activity in the Americas, partly offset by better performance in Asia and the U.S. The company also cited unfavorable product mix in Asia and the Americas, which pressured margins even as volumes in certain pockets held up.

Tariff Dynamics are Dilutive

Tariff recoveries added revenue, with about $15 million in Q4 and roughly $38 million for the full year, but these flows are margin-dilutive because they come with little incremental profit. Management expects tariffs to be broadly breakeven in 2026, meaning they are unlikely to provide meaningful upside to margins even if they support the top line.

One-Off and Separation Expenses

Separation-related transaction and finalization costs are still visible in the adjusted free cash flow bridge, though they are tapering and should fade over time. Some of the recent tax reductions were also one-time in nature, implying that future cash taxes will normalize at a somewhat higher but still improved run-rate level.

Modest Near-Term Volume Outlook

Looking across its end markets, PHINIA expects global industry volumes, including battery electric vehicles, to be flat to slightly down in 2026, limiting organic tailwinds. As a result, the company’s growth algorithm leans heavily on market share gains, product wins, and mix shifts toward higher-value content rather than robust underlying demand.

2026 Financial Guidance and Outlook

For 2026, PHINIA guided net sales to a range of $3.5 billion to $3.7 billion, implying mid-single-digit growth including FX and low-single-digit growth excluding currency, with about a two-point FX benefit and tariffs assumed neutral. Adjusted EBITDA is projected at $485 million to $525 million with a 13.7% to 14.3% margin, adjusted free cash flow of $200 million to $240 million, an adjusted tax rate of 30% to 34%, and around 20% EBITDA conversion on incremental revenue.

PHINIA’s earnings call painted a picture of a company executing well operationally, defending margins against multiple headwinds, and returning substantial capital even in a low-growth environment. With strategic wins in aerospace and alternative fuels, a healthier tax structure, and a cautious but positive 2026 guide, the story is one of disciplined progress rather than dramatic acceleration, which may appeal to investors seeking steady cash generation and buyback support.

Anheuser‑Busch InBev’s latest earnings call struck a broadly positive tone, balancing solid profit, margin and cash‑return progress with frank acknowledgment of volume softness and China‑specific challenges. Underlying EPS and Profit Growth

Underlying EPS reached $3.73, up 6% in U.S. dollars and 9.4% in constant currency, adding $350 million in underlying profit. Management noted that dollar EPS has compounded at 6.7% annually since 2021, underscoring resilient earnings growth despite uneven demand and currency volatility.

EBITDA rose 4.9% year over year, with a 101 basis‑point expansion in margins as four of five regions improved profitability. The company highlighted disciplined cost control and mix upgrades as key drivers, signaling that it sees additional room for margin gains even if volume growth remains subdued.

Topline and Revenue‑per‑Unit Improvement

Revenue per hectoliter climbed 4.4%, powering a 2% increase in overall revenue despite weaker volumes in several markets. AB InBev said it grew revenue in 65% of its markets, pointing to pricing, premiumization and mix as effective levers in a pressured consumer backdrop.

Growth in Beyond Beer and Non‑Alcohol Portfolio

Beyond Beer revenue jumped 23% while the non‑alcohol beer portfolio surged 34%, with non‑alcohol gaining share in roughly 70% of its top 14 markets. Management framed these segments as structural growth engines, capturing changing consumer tastes and diversifying away from traditional beer cycles.

Digital Ecosystem Momentum — BEES and DTC

The BEES B2B platform processed $53 billion in GMV, up 12%, while its Marketplace layer grew GMV 61% to $3.5 billion. On the consumer side, direct‑to‑consumer users reached 12.3 million, up 11%, reinforcing AB InBev’s pitch that its digital ecosystem is deepening customer relationships and driving efficiency.

Free Cash Flow, Buybacks and Balance Sheet

Strong free cash flow funded $3.2 billion of share repurchases and supports an ongoing $6 billion buyback program, alongside $2.7 billion of debt repurchases. Leverage improved to 2.87x despite a $2.8 billion FX headwind, and the board proposed a €1 per‑share final dividend, lifting the total dividend 15% year over year.

Mega Brands, Innovation and Premiumization

AB InBev’s mega brands have grown revenue at a 10% CAGR since 2021 and now account for 57% of company sales, reflecting a continued premiumization push. Innovation across packaging, new brands and liquids contributed 11% of 2025 revenue, with Corona volumes doubling since 2018 and rising double digits in 30 markets in 2025.

Regional Operational Performance Highlights

In the U.S., the company gained share in beer and spirits, led by Michelob ULTRA and Busch Light, while Cutwater delivered triple‑digit growth. Mexico posted mid‑single‑digit top‑ and bottom‑line gains, Colombia delivered double‑digit EBITDA growth, Brazil returned to volume growth in December and South Africa grew revenue and EBITDA mid‑single digits.

Overall Volume Underperformance

Management admitted that 2025 volumes lagged both expectations and historical trends in many regions, with much of the year characterized by underperformance. Momentum improved only in December and early 2026, suggesting that the recovery is still in its early stages and remains vulnerable to swings in demand.

China Revenue and Volume Decline

China was a notable weak spot, with revenue down low teens in 2025 and volumes trailing a relatively stable industry due to inventory adjustments and channel shifts. While Q4 share trends stabilized versus the prior year, the company stressed that rebuilding momentum in China will take time and targeted operational focus.

FX, Hedging and Cost Headwinds

A stronger euro created a $2.8 billion FX headwind on net debt and higher hedging costs partly offset lower interest expense, pressuring financials. Management also flagged transactional FX and hedge timing as key risks, particularly in the first half of 2026, which could distort reported earnings and margins.

Consumer, Weather and On‑Trade Pressures

AB InBev cited a constrained consumer environment, inflation‑driven disposable‑income stress and unseasonal weather, including cold, rainy conditions in Brazil, as near‑term demand drags. In China, on‑trade remains weak and past anti‑extravagance policies plus regional differences forced distribution changes that have weighed on growth.

Phasing and Near‑Term Cost Pressures

The company warned that hedge timing and planned marketing spend around major events like the World Cup could create quarter‑to‑quarter margin volatility in 2026. Investors were cautioned not to overreact to short‑term phasing, as management insists that underlying profitability trends remain intact.

Forward‑Looking Guidance and Outlook

For 2026, AB InBev guided to 4–8% organic EBITDA growth, capex of $3.5–4.0 billion and a normalized tax rate of 26–28%, aligned with its medium‑term framework. It plans to keep leaning on premiumization, digital platforms and disciplined capital returns, while acknowledging FX, China and consumer softness as ongoing watchpoints.

AB InBev’s call blended confidence in its earnings engine, digital scale and brand power with realism about volumes and China‑related hurdles. For investors, the story is one of steady EPS and margin progress, robust cash returns and improving momentum, tempered by near‑term FX, cost phasing and regional execution risks that will shape 2026 trading.

This pricing and mix discipline helped offset weak volumes and underpinned the margin gains seen in both the consolidated business and the Brazil Beer unit.

BEES Marketplace: Scale and Profitability

The BEES B2B marketplace remained a standout growth engine, posting a 70% jump in full‑year GMV. Importantly, BEES gross margin improved by 3.5 percentage points, signaling that the platform is not just scaling but also becoming structurally more profitable for Ambev and its trade partners.

Zé Delivery: Direct Reach to Younger Consumers

Zé Delivery continued to deepen Ambev’s connection with end consumers, particularly younger cohorts. The platform generated BRL 4.7 billion in GMV, up 13% year on year, processed 67 million orders, and grew yearly active users 11% to 27 million, with about 80% of buyers coming from Gen Z and millennial segments.

Premium, Balanced, and Nonalcohol Portfolio Wins

Ambev’s portfolio strategy paid off where consumers traded up or across categories, with premium and super premium volumes growing in the high teens. Balanced‑choice brands surged in the high‑60% range, while nonalcohol volumes advanced about 30% and in the fourth quarter the company captured an estimated 100% of Brazilian beer industry growth in premium and nonalcohol.

Cash Generation and Aggressive Shareholder Returns

Operating cash flow reached BRL 24.5 billion, giving Ambev ample firepower for both investment and capital returns. Management returned BRL 21.7 billion to shareholders on a cash basis, roughly 90% of operating cash flow, and laid out a 2025 program of about BRL 20 billion in dividends, interest on capital, and share buybacks.

Cost Discipline and Operational Efficiency

Cost control remained a key pillar as Brazil Beer cash COGS per hectoliter, excluding marketplace products, rose 6.1% yet still landed in the lowest quartile of prior guidance. Productivity initiatives and industrial and logistics efficiencies helped, while SG&A growth stayed contained even as Ambev continued to fund marketing and brand support.

Volume Headwinds and Industry Weakness

Despite solid pricing and mix, Ambev faced broad‑based volume pressure across its footprint, driven largely by cyclical and occasion‑related factors. Prolonged cold weather linked to La Niña made October particularly weak, and although trends improved in the fourth quarter and December, full‑year volumes remained under strain.

Core Beer Segment Under Pressure

The core beer segment felt the brunt of the downturn due to its heavy exposure to out‑of‑home consumption and social occasions. This dynamic caused share pressure earlier in the year and forced Ambev to ramp up trade activation and marketing efforts, which helped recover share into the fourth quarter but did not fully offset the earlier drag.

Brazil NAB: Profitability Versus Market Share

In Brazil’s non‑alcoholic beverages, management leaned into revenue management decisions that supported profitability but slowed volume momentum. As a result, the NAB business suffered volume deceleration in the second half and some market share pressure versus its stronger first‑half performance.

FX Losses and Higher Net Financial Expenses

Below the operating line, Ambev’s results were hit by nearly BRL 4 billion in net financial expenses for the year. The increase was driven largely by foreign‑exchange variation losses on foreign‑currency‑denominated assets and higher costs linked to sourcing U.S. dollars in Bolivia.

Working Capital Drag on Cash Flow

While cash generation stayed strong overall, cash flow from operating activities fell BRL 1.6 billion year on year to BRL 24.5 billion, mainly due to softer volumes pressuring working capital. Financing activities consumed BRL 26.8 billion, reflecting heavy shareholder payouts and the completion of a previous share buyback program.

Regional Soft Spots: Argentina and Dominican Republic

Not all geographies participated equally in the recovery narrative, with Argentina’s consumption rebound moving slower than management had hoped despite sequential improvement. The Dominican Republic also saw a setback in the fourth quarter as adverse weather disrupted performance, adding to the broader volume headwinds.

2026 Outlook: Costs Up, Margins Still in Focus

Looking ahead, Ambev guided Brazil Beer cash COGS per hectoliter, excluding non‑Ambev marketplace products, to increase 4.5% to 7.5% in 2026 on commodity, aluminum, and mix pressures. Even so, management reiterated a commitment to expand consolidated EBITDA margins over time, supported by disciplined capital allocation, ongoing digital growth, and continued revenue per hectoliter gains.

Ambev’s earnings call painted a picture of a brewer that is absorbing meaningful external shocks yet still protecting profitability and returning substantial cash to shareholders. Investors will now watch whether easing weather and macro conditions, combined with the premium and digital strategies, can translate into a clearer volume recovery as the company navigates another year of cost headwinds.

Network Upgrades Target Higher Speeds and Capacity

Liberty Broadband is advancing DOCSIS 4.0‑capable HFC and wireless upgrades, already offering 2.5 Gbps in areas with fiber middle mile in place. In Anchorage, the company is upgrading its 1.8 GHz core and reducing node sizes, laying the groundwork to scale speeds to 5 Gbps and beyond and positioning the network for future data demand.

Alaska Build‑outs and BEAD Awards Support Expansion

The company completed its iHUC one net build‑out to deliver 2.5 Gbps service to Delta, marking a key milestone in its Alaska expansion plan. It has also been provisionally awarded about $120 million in BEAD funds by the State of Alaska, which, once approved, would offset a significant portion of the capital costs of extending broadband to underserved areas.

Operational Resilience Tested by Extreme Events

Management highlighted rapid restoration efforts after major outages, including repairing a Dutch Harbor fiber break in less than two weeks and fully restoring Typhoon Helong‑impacted villages in under four months. Fiber service on a third‑party network was also fully restored during 2025, demonstrating the company’s capability to respond to severe disruptions, albeit at a cost.

Data Subscriber Losses Reflect Intensifying Competition

Data subscribers fell 3% year over year to 151,200, with a loss of 4,500 subscribers over the year and 1,200 in the fourth quarter, underscoring churn pressures. Management cited wireless substitution, competition from providers like Starlink, and fallout from a third‑party fiber break as key drivers of the decline.

Consumer Revenue Faces Headwinds Despite Wireless Growth

Consumer revenue declined 2% for the full year as the shutdown of the legacy video business and data subscriber losses weighed on the top line. These pressures were only partly offset by growth in wireless revenue and higher federal wireless subsidies, highlighting the importance of converged offerings to stabilize consumer results.

Service Interruptions Add Cost and Reputation Risk

December brought two significant fiber breaks in Dutch Harbor and Dearing, with Dutch Harbor resolved quickly but Dearing repairs pushed to summer due to ice conditions. Management expects repair costs in the low single‑digit millions, adding near‑term expense and raising the bar for customer retention in affected markets.

Promotions Power Postpaid Growth but Raise Questions

Postpaid wireless growth was materially driven by promotions, including an unlimited test drive and a twelve‑month free offer, which successfully boosted line counts. Early retention trends are described as strong, but the dependence on heavy promotions, alongside ongoing prepaid and lifeline erosion, suggests that sustainable organic growth remains a work in progress.

BEAD Funding Still Subject to Approval and Timing Risk

While the provisional BEAD award of roughly $120 million is a potential tailwind, it remains subject to federal approval and state negotiations that could affect timing and size. This uncertainty complicates capital planning for the expansion program, even as management assumes that grants will ultimately reduce the out‑of‑pocket spend on rural projects.

CapEx Peak in 2026 to Pressure Near‑Term Free Cash Flow

Management flagged 2026 as a peak investment year, with expected capital expenditures of about $290 million, including roughly $20 million carried over from 2025, versus 2025 CapEx of $224 million net of grants. The company signaled that this elevated spend will drive proportionately lower free cash flow in 2026, before dropping back toward a more normal 15–20% of revenue range longer term.

Leverage Manageable but Under Watch as Spending Rises

Total debt stands at about $1.0 billion, with net leverage under the credit agreement at 2.3x and consolidated net leverage at 1.6x after factoring in parent cash and preferred stock. These metrics remain reasonable, yet investors will be watching closely as CapEx ramps to ensure the balance sheet stays flexible and interest costs remain contained.

Customer Win‑Back After Outages Proves Slow

Despite restoration of third‑party fiber service, management acknowledged that winning back customers in outage‑affected areas has been slower than hoped, signaling lingering trust and switching‑cost issues. This slow recovery suggests that certain markets could face longer‑term revenue drag, making reliability and communication key priorities alongside network upgrades.

Guidance Points to Stable 2026 Amid Investment Cycle

Looking ahead, management characterized 2026 as “stable,” built on the foundation of record 2025 results, ample cash of $429 million, roughly $1.0 billion of debt, and $377 million in undrawn revolver capacity. With 2026 CapEx projected at around $290 million, the company expects lower free cash flow next year as it funds DOCSIS 4.0 and 5G‑ready upgrades, completes Alaska build‑outs, and selectively deploys rights‑offering proceeds, while aiming for a return to more normalized investment levels over time.

Liberty Broadband’s earnings call painted a picture of a company in the middle of a heavy but purposeful investment cycle, balancing solid current profitability with ambitious network upgrades and rural expansion. Record financial metrics, strong liquidity, and promising BEAD grants provide support, but investors must weigh these positives against subscriber losses, outage‑related challenges, and a temporary dip in free cash flow as CapEx peaks in 2026.
